nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q1.alt: Question 1. The first illustration best helps the reader understand the information in which paragraph? Choices: A: paragraph 2 B: paragraph 4 C: paragraph 5 D: paragraph 7

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q2.alt: Question 2. Read the chart below. SUGARING STEPS. Step 1: Syrup makers tap trees. Step 2 is blank. Step 3: Syrup makers collect sap in buckets. Which statement best completes the chart? Choices: A: Syrup makers store syrup in tanks. B: Syrup makers boil sap in large pans. C: Syrup makers put spouts into drilled holes. D: Syrup makers pour sap into gathering tanks.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q3.alt: Question 3. According to the Filtering section, why do syrup makers remove minerals from maple syrup? Choices: A: Minerals make the syrup grainy. B: Minerals change the syrup’s taste. C: Minerals change the syrup’s color. D: Minerals stop the syrup from flowing.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q4.alt: Question 4. Which section of the article contains information about the process of turning sap into syrup? Choices: A: Tapping the Trees B: In the Sugarhouse C: Filtering D: Color Grading

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q5.alt: Question 5. Which sentence best states what the reader learns in paragraph 8? Choices: A: The otters are fun-loving animals. B: Maggie has been hoping to see the otters. C: Maggie makes friends with a mother otter. D: The otters are seen next to the floating logs.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q6.alt: Question 6. What do paragraphs 16 and 17 show about otters and gulls? Choices: A: Gulls and otters compete for the same food. B: Otters watch to see if gulls are nearby. C: Gulls and otters avoid each other. D: Gulls sometimes tease otters.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q7.alt: Question 7. Which detail from the story best shows why Maggie believes the otter trusts her? Choices: A: “Swiftly, a young brown otter swam toward the still figure on the edge of the cliff.” (paragraph 6) B: “Two otters wrestled, tumbling over and over.” (paragraph 8) C: “The otter was gone.” (paragraph 17) D: “And when he had eaten, he twisted in the water and chased his tail.” (paragraph 31)

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q8.alt: Question 8. Read paragraphs 26 and 27 from the story. When Friday afternoon came, she burst into the house and grabbed her fishing pole. “What a girl for fishing!” her mother said. Which sentence best explains what causes Maggie’s mother to say this? Choices: A: “Maggie sat down near the edge of the low, rocky cliff and baited her hook.” (paragraph 1) B: “A fish flopped up over the rocks.” (paragraph 11) C: “Each day after school, all the following week, Maggie fished and watched.” (paragraph 24) D: “Mom, I forgot to fish.” (paragraph 34)

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q9.alt: Question 9. Which sentence best describes a main idea of the story? Choices: A: “Maggie sat down near the edge of the low, rocky cliff and baited her hook. The beach was empty. Not even Barnaby was in sight.” (paragraph 1) B: “A small herd of otters rocked with the roll of the waves, cradled in blankets of kelp, forepaws folded on their chests.” (paragraph 5) C: “Maggie waited. And there he was again. This time with a mussel in one paw and a rock in the other.” (paragraph 18) D: “‘A tame otter,’ Maggie said. ‘He’s really friendly. He’s as playful as a puppy, and he trusts me.’” (paragraph 38)

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q10.alt: Question 10. Read paragraph 3 from the passage. Teachers say snowshoes are improving their students’: hearts and lungs, muscles, and spirits. What does “improving their students’ . . . spirits” most likely mean? Choices: A: The students study harder. B: The students can walk farther. C: The students are more curious. D: The students are much happier.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q11.alt: Question 11. Which paragraph explains how snowshoeing affects the body? Choices: A: paragraph 9 B: paragraph 11 C: paragraph 13 D: paragraph 15

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q12.alt: Question 12. In paragraph 15, the word “hike” most closely means Choices: A: a planned walk B: a relay race C: an indoor activity D: an outdoor class

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. Which statement from the passage is explained by the information in paragraph 17? Choices: A: “Snowshoes are huge.” (paragraph 14) B: “Then they hit the slopes and trails nearby.” (paragraph 15) C: “It’s not unusual to see animal signs.” (paragraph 15) D: “The rabbit that made them had something in common with the kids.” (paragraph 16)

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. How do the details in paragraph 18 support a main idea of the passage? Choices: A: The details show ways that kids observe the weather. B: The details give examples of kids enjoying the snow. C: The details give evidence that the kids get lots of exercise. D: The details show why the kids are learning about nature.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. How does the information in the section titled “Snowshoe Smarts” add to the information in the rest of the passage? Choices: A: The section gives instructions on how to be a good trail-breaker. B: The section gives suggestions for people who want to go snowshoeing. C: The section gives explanations for some of the words used in the passage. D: The section gives details about the snowshoeing program at the elementary school.

nysed-ela-2014-g3-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. Which statement about snowshoes is made clearer by the photograph at the end of the passage? Choices: A: Students must lift their feet up when walking in snowshoes. B: Students study winter wildlife while wearing snowshoes. C: Walking in snowshoes takes a lot of energy. D: Snowshoes are very large and flat.
